G. Stroppini, "Amour, dialogue et unité dans l'oeuvre de
Virgile ( Bucoliques , Géorgiques, Énéide I-IV)," LEC 65 (1997) 97-
115.
Foster's study of the hero's name accents the associations of Aineias
with ainos "dread, terrible," and ainos "tale, story" (cf. epainos
praise, fame). Aen. 12, 938-9, acer in armis balances 12, 946-7,
ira/terribilis. Pietas, troublesome to critics, is part of the hero's ainos /
ainigmos. Knox studies the applications of the epithet saevus/saeva in
gods and heroes of the epic. Inter alia, Aeneas shares the savage grief
of Juno at 12, 945. Stroppini studies the omnipresence of the duality
and mutability of love in V.'s poetry and argues for a less pessimistic
reading.

raw_text:
Oleg Nikitinski, "Zu Vergil Aen. 10, 366," RhM 139
(1996) 191-192.
Dyson argues that Vergil chose to be inconsistent regarding septima
aestas in order to highlight the theme of sacrificial death. Harrison is
convinced that the Caesar of Aen. 1,286 is unambiguously Augustus.
Bliss interprets Aen. 2, 311-312, proximus ardet/Ucalegon, a detail in
Vergil's reference to two houses, one of a Trojan councillor, lit. "Mr
What-me-worry?", the other of Deiphobus, a son of Priam. Both are
enveloped in destructive fire, and indicate "the incomprehensible
justice of what must be interpreted as the will of heaven" (Austin,
Oxford 1964, ix). Vergil realized that a catastrophe would consume
"virtuous, wicked, wise and foolish" indiscriminately. Egan suggests
that if Aeneas did not really see Helen cowering at the Vesta temple, the
removal of the "Helen episode" is unnecessary. The verses are
authentic. Hardy reassesses Anchises' inaccurate reading of the Delian
riddle; the Penates provide the corrective reading: the human mother
(Crete) has to be abandoned for the promise of a destined motherland
(Italia). Khan argues that hospitium and pietas are common to all the
main episodes of Aeneas' Odyssey, and that there is an intriguing
relationship between the "political" episodes involving the Harpies
and Achaemenides. Both deal with an enemy, but in dramatically
different terms. Fernandelli assesses the solemn funeral of Polydorus as
a Euripidean element and as closure to the Iloupersis . Deschamps
vindicates Somnus as promoter of Palinurus to the status of hero.
O'Hara counters Cockwood's article ( Phoenix 46 (1992) 362-365) and
textual emendation to have Aeneas exit through the gate of true
dreams; the received text (however cryptic) should remain intact.
Manetti detects a person rather than a transmitter in Deiphobe.
Cristofoli assumes pius amor (in the Epicurean sense) between Euryalus
and Nisus, Salius and Patronus, Elymus and Panope. Nikitinski prefers
aspera aquis natura over aspera equis natura (Aen. 10, 366).

raw_text:
Stephen Christopher Smith, Perspective and Prophecy: The
Uses of Achilles in the Aeneid. Diss., University of Virginia, 1996.
Álvarez pleads for the composite unity of the Dido episode; Book 4
cannot be isolated; it is neither tragedy nor epyllion. Dyson's article
examines Dido's shifting association with Epicureanism: the philosophy of ataraxy was finally inadequate because it could neither
explain nor combat the reality of pain and evil. Michels explored the
"bigness" and "figura" of Aeneas in the eyes of others who visualize
the hero according to their own taste not according to physical characteristics. Polk senses a partial Penelope in Dido: "but Dido weakens and
falls, bringing down her kingdom, while Penelope resists." (49)

Marianne Wifstrand Schiebe, Vergil und die Tradition von
den römischen Urkönigen [Hermes-Einzelschrift 76] Stuttgart 1997.
Johnston studies poetic descriptions of volcanic activity and its causes
as preface to Vergil's descriptions and allusive remarks on volcanoes
(and volcanic personalities) in the Georgics and the Aeneid. Schiebe' s
in-depth study treats Evander, Faunus, Janus, Latinus, Picus, and with
particular zeal, Saturn.

raw_text:
E. Stärk, Kampanien als geistige Landschaft. Interpretationen zum antiken Bild des Golfs von Neapel. [Zetemata 95]
München 1995. Rev: Voit, Gymnasium 104 (1997) 183-185.
Caputo' s guide to Cumae and its environs is a cleverly designed and
lavishly illustrated companion for scholars and visitors. A review
appears in Vergilius 43 (1997). Clark studies Cumaean-Avernian
dwelling places of the Sibyl, the (pseudo-) Grotta della Sibilla' s
progress from military tunnel to Sibyl's cave, and its mediaeval
conversion into a public therapeutic spa, and offers a meticulous study
of the responses of Conrad of Querfurt, Bishop of Hildesheim (1195),
and of Petrarch (1343) to the locale. Clark also provides a thematic,
syntactic and lexical study of the description of the Sibyl's Cave by
Agathias in Byzantine times, when it was a casualty of the siege by
Narses. Franklin's compendium of Vergilian graffiti at Pompeii
provides a valuable teaching resource. Paliotti's "popular" article is
richly illustrated with color plates and line-drawings of architectural
reconstructions of the Cumaean acropolis and lower city, and of the
Roman forum, replete with Domitianic Capitolium, Aedes of the
Augustales, and Antonine Baths.

David West , Horace Odes I: Carpe Diem. Oxford 1995.
Rev: Lowrie, Vergilius 42 (1996) 148-151.
Doblhofer underscores Vergilian (and Horatian) responses to imperial
expansion and their relevance to British imperialism. Fabre-Serris
traces echoes of the polemic between Vergil and Gallus over the
Hellenistic vogue of amoris dolores in Bucolics 2 and 10, with
reverberations in Propertius 1, 20, and in Ovid's Metamorphoses.
Huxley's address examines the Ovidian hexameter, the Eclogues, where
basic differences surface between the Vergilian and Ovidian approach,
the Georgics, where Vergil's genius is paramount and distinct (the
poets' handling of the Orpheus and Eurydice legend is instructive), and
Ovid's "potted version" of the Aeneid where pietas and Dido vie for
attention. Finally, on Ovid's style, Huxley offers a reproof to Ovid's
detractors who "blame him for extravagance in language which is
judged far more sympathetically if it occurs in Virgil, or because Ovid
is usually so limpid and crystal-clear that they dismiss the very
possibility of hidden depths." (102). Varius Rufus has captured the
spotlight several times: Leigh's "reading" of Varius' Thyestes as
applicable to Marc Antony, is provocative and instructive. Mazzoni' s
"reconstruction" of the historical Anser is masterful. Nagore de Zand

raw_text:
M. К. Thornton, "Vergil's Inj
Aeneid," Latomus 55 (1996) 389-393.
Barchiesi and Edmunds provide introductory and exploratory manoeuvers with intertextuality in Roman epic and elsewhere. Both offer
guidelines and caution for enthusiastic critics. Desy returns to the
Laudes Italiae ( Georgics 2,136-176) and to the War in Italy (Aeri. 10)
and detects ironic undertones and subversive criticism of Octavian' s
"victories" in Aeneas' behavior. Huxley's eloquence matches his
theme. Parker treats Vergil's exploitation of the book endings, the
compulsory pauses between Books 4 & 5, 1 and 2, 8 and 9. Pontes'
detailed analysis of Aeneid 6, 122-123 is a tour de force and entirely
convincing. Thornton studies the sympathetic implications of the
injured deer motif for Dido and Turnus; the innate cruelty of the
individual is accented by the act of injuring the blameless deer.

Dyer's conclusions are brisk and sensible, that "Vergil seems to have
had the poet Parthenius of Nicaea as his grammaticus, at least in Greek,
during his education in Cremona in Transpadane Gaul, where
Parthenius probably at the end of the Mithridatic wars was a slave in the
household of the poet C. Helvius Cinna." (23) Ruiz de Elvira , critical
of conjectural criticism , vindicates the authenticity of the datum which
is evidenced first in Pliny the Elder.
